speaker: interviewer
text: September, 1995. So, Ruth, you decided that you just had to-- he was the only person you knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only hope. Because the whole world I-- I knew, they chased me. I knew they-- I had to take the risk to go back to him and see what happened. And it was already 10 days. And nobody thought of asking about me by him. And they were quite changed, enormously friendly. Prepared-- she prepared food for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: She?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miss-- his wife, which was actually the softer of them two. And she said to me, look, have a good meal with us. Go, have a good bath. Lie down. And I have a nice surprise for you tomorrow. Don't you worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So their attitude towards you, in the-- in the-- in the 10 days or so, 8 days since you had last been there--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Changed-- changed completely. Because they thought that they will have the police chasing me there and nothing to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So no link was made--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --between you and them. And they felt safer?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Because nobody actually in the camp-- I had never said that I had an outside contact. So it would be a miracle that somebody would have found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so at this time the attitude was different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely different. And she said to me, you know-- when I was first time there, I asked them whether they would take a letter to my brother from me, that he knows that I run away. And she said, they were reading the letter. And it broke their heart. And they're so happy that I am here, because they have a brother of his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not far in the country, who has a few acres. And he has a little homestead. And he is very tough that Ukrainians can't stay by him. Everybody runs away, because he has so high demands. But they know that the very moment, he looks for somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looks-- meaning looking for help, for work, workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: For workers. And they gave me his address. But, of course, please don't mention-- and also, I told him about the Hamburg experience. That I think that it's a good start for me to say I came there. And so I used-- I went there to this little-- it's even not township. It just had a few acres of land and a few little huts you can call them. They were peasant homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this place have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Muhlatschutz. Muhlatschutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Muhlatschutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was. It was, you know, only a few homesteads were there. And I came there. The house was open, nobody's there. And then when it started to get a bit dark, they came from the fields. They worked until it is dark. And I-- I said, look, I am looking for work. I worked in Hamburg. But Hamburg was bombarded. And we were told, just see where you can find work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they looked at me. And I was so terribly thin. And they said, but Fraulinechen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] will you be able to work in the fields? And I said, oh, I'm very strong. [LAUGHS] Anyways, so he sent me next day with her out. And-- Her? Who was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wife, Mrs. also [? Wagner, ?] because it was the brother of the fellow. And I saw what she did it, so I did the same. And sort of they kept me. And I was fed the first time. And they were astonished that I eat their meals up, as it was a terrible thing. Today, I couldn't look at it. But I was so happy I had it. Because I was so hungry. And they sent me with them. I went to the fields and milked the cows and did cleaning. And one thing, he-- he loved to work in the fields. But to write in his accounts out when his supply, his potatoes-- that was for him terrible. And I-- I told him, you know, I'm quite good in writing. So I made all his accounts out. And for me it was better than the working in the fields. And so it went for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say a while, how long do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it could have been also a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let me just ask you also, Muhlatschutz, the little village, where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was by Oels or Breslau. It was also in Schlesien. And there, actually, there was one policeman for two such little townships. And I-- I think something with my papers I already started with this policeman, but it wasn't much. It wasn't complete, not all I needed to really, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you tried to get papers for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the story you used was that bombed out Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is exactly right. I worked in a household and maked-- made up a story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked in the fields a bit, you did the accounts, you were--?

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- for him what was writing, I always, can I do it? Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were well fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Relatively well fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: --today, I wouldn't touch the stuff. Because Germans have-- are terrible cooks, and especially in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What food did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called [GERMAN]. It was a soup made out of flour, I suppose milk flour and boiled potatoes cut in. That was breakfast. So would you eat that? But I-- I was absolutely thrilled with it, because I was so hungry. And he-- he said, you see, you're so good [INAUDIBLE] here Ukrainian, since they didn't want to eat it. [LAUGHS] So then I [INAUDIBLE] maybe. I look too [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Aryan, you know. I always was so light, blonde. And big blue eyes I had. And there was in the next, not village, but the house next to them, there was one help like I was a help for them. So there was a male help. And this male help wanted to go out with me when we had once a fortnight, I had the Sunday afternoon I could go out. So I couldn't shake him off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he from? Was he a local--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Pole. No, a Pole, a Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Polish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When he have been a German, there wouldn't have been any danger. But the Poles smell Jews. They have a nose for it that is absolutely impossible. Anyway, so he must-- I don't know which way it came to this Mr. Wagner. And he had a talk to me. He said, oh, [INAUDIBLE], whatever his name was said you could be Jewish. You don't want to go out with him. I don't want any trouble. And you know you're anyway very weak in the work. And I really need a man. I'll give you address from the [NON-ENGLISH], that is like is a-- where they supply work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Labor office.

speaker: interviewee
text: Labor office. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you just see what you can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- this Polish fellow--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --really took revenge on you. You wouldn't go out with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is-- that's exactly right. And you know, because going out, and they are very primitive people. And I wanted to stay away from that. And when I went to this-- to this-- [NON-ENGLISH], where they supply the work, I was lucky again. The girl at the counter, I shortly told her the story. I said, he needs a man. I'm too weak. And actually I worked in the household. And I'm trained for homework.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am an excellent cook, which I was, and whether she would be good enough to exchange me. And she looked at me, and maybe she smelled something. Because she said, look, have you got an [NON-ENGLISH]? I said, no. [NON-ENGLISH] ?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means for working document.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were the papers, the work papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When you had said, you sort of didn't have any worries anymore. You always could show when somebody asks you about papers. You show-- showed that. And that was good enough. So she did that for me. And she said, I have very good friends--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, she did what for you? She asked--

speaker: interviewee
text: This--

speaker: interviewer
text: --she asked you if you had an [NON-ENGLISH]. And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, no. Because I lost everything from Hamburg. So she said, we-- I fix you an [NON-ENGLISH]. And I have good friends in the station restaurant in Oels. And they're looking for somebody who helps them. And we send you there. I said, sounds fantastic. And I said that it will be very nice of you. So I had to finish with the peasants to bring in the corn. And that is always very hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the harvest.

speaker: interviewee
text: The harvest. So if he wanted that I finish the harvest, which I did with him. And then I took whatever I had, my little bag, and went to Oels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this woman actually gave you a workbook? You had--

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave me a workbook. And after the war, I met a girl, and she-- who was also in Oels, worked on the Post Office. And she said to me, you know that Mrs. So-and-so had a Jewish husband. And she helped Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you think she-- she-- she knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: She felt it in her heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she decided to help you and give you a workbook?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Because for me, she was like, you know, German official. And I wouldn't have asked her what her name is and so on. I was just immensely happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- this workbook she gave you really was a passport to life?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is exactly, yes. You can say that. And also, the position she gave me was fantastic. Because the couple I came to, the owners of this restaurant, they were very refined elderly German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Roter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And they were so enormously nice to me. I wasn't hungry anymore. I was in a restaurant. Food was there. And I came as a washing plate. I had to wash all of the plates, plates and coffee cups and plates and coffee cups. I didn't see anything more, but the plates and the coffee cups. And so that went on for a little while. And then I was always happy to be in the restaurant, not to go out, not to be seen. And night time, I served coffee, only-- only coffee. Through-- for the restaurant through the window I prepared it. And they distributed it to the tables. And one night, the old of Mrs. Roter came into the kitchen. And she said, Toni I have visitors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Toni?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the name. My Polish name was too hard for them to pronounce. So they just that was the name they gave me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Polish name that you took, that you gave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yes. I thought not to forget my own name, I took the name of my Polish teacher. And I thought, that I can't forget. So that was [INAUDIBLE]. Couldn't be more Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so when you needed a name in the labor office? Or, was this even-- this was much earlier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Much earlier. I-- as soon as--

speaker: interviewer
text: After you escaped from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, as soon as I escaped, I-- I said that is my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. So you became, as soon as you got out of the [NON-ENGLISH], you were [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That is right. That is what I told myself. And I-- I adopted myself to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did the people call you Toni, not [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because [INAUDIBLE] was too hard for them. Toni was more a common name. And they said, we call you Toni. And I said, fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were Toni?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was Tony. And I was a bit careful in this restaurant first. I didn't speak my proper German. I spoke like a Pole speaks, you know? Because I thought when I speak too well German, that is suspicious. So I fixed it a bit up. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, till the point when my boss lady came in and said, oh, I don't know what to do. I can't cook. And I have some important visitors. What will we do? And then I said, if you don't mind, I can cook. What? What is it what you want? What you have? Because it was all restricted also there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember with all my knowledge from the household school, I made a beautiful wiener schnitzel with mashed potatoes, and veggies. And I set the table wonderful up. And she was happy. And the next day, he came in the kitchen and said, Toni, we will prepare you. We will teach you. You will be the main cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all your finishing school knowledge helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helped me. Helped me tremendously. And I was taught by the Ukrainian who was before the main cook, what to do and how to do it. And she was pregnant. She was happy that she could leave. And I don't know what happened to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: The cook-- the cook was pregnant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainian cook was pregnant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And from then on, I made my way so that I get the contact with my late brother, which was very important for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had somehow managed to know all the time that your brother was alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and where he was. How did you manage that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through this Wagner. I didn't know at the time when-- when my-- when we were in camp that he was an SS man. Otherwise, he couldn't have found out that. But he found out for me where he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- that was a concentration camp, Funfeichen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was still in Funfeichen all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how did you create contact now with your brother once you were working at the restaurant in Oels? How did you--?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? How I got in contact with him? In the restaurant when I was working, there was a fellow who did all the shoppings for all the station. Not only our restaurant, but for everybody who was employed at the station. And he came every day. He must have a chicken. Or, he came for potato peels for the animals. And I looked at him. And I said, oh, that is a nice old man. And I took a bit of meat and a bit of sugar. And I put it into the potato peels. And I thought if they catch me, I said, it fall in. It could have fallen in. And he appreciated it greatly. And we became very friendly. And I told him I have-- I have a brother. There were also these volunteer working camps, you know. I have a brother in the camp. And I want to send him parcels if he would help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you told this old man who used to come for the vegetable peelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I have a brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --who was not a-- not a Jewish kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a Jewish brother, a brother-- a brother who was a volunteer worker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I can't remember today, was it my brother gave me the name of a Frenchman? The Frenchman were-- they were forced labor, but they were free to move. And so such a Frenchman, I made contact with. And all-- I stole, because I stole it, all the food. And all-- all-- even clothes I-- I cut. There were like little tickets for the meals. So when the Germans gave me a ticket for 200 grams of meat cut in their meal, I cut 150. So 50 I kept for me. And I gave that [INAUDIBLE]. And so I had food, meat, for my brother. And for the Frenchman, I took cigarettes, which I sold to the Germans. But I [INAUDIBLE] when I was a cook, I had every thing open there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through this elderly man who took the peelings--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had contact with my brother. I created--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Frenchman was a-- a forced laborer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I never saw him. That all-- all went--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- and the Frenchman was sort of the channel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was a channel. He worked together with my brother in-- in this camp where he was. And so it went for quite a while. And what I did nighttime, I worked on the counter, served beer and things like that. And when I had Germans there, the soldiers, and they looked to me a bit like soft-hearted. I said, I'm so cold. I would need a pair of socks, since it's so hard to get. So they brought me socks. And I sent it to my brother, that he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had something.

speaker: interviewee
text: --had something

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let me just ask you a little bit about the restaurant itself. The restaurant was in Oels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oel, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a railway station restaurant?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a railway station and very lively. Because from all directions, they changed there on that station. Trains--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so Oels was a railway junction.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a junction, exactly. And they served coffee. And lunchtime were hot meals. And nighttime, they served again coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was constant movement of German troops through there? There were soldiers all the time coming and going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soldiers and civilians, too. Everybody, everybody. And yes, maybe that is something [INAUDIBLE] in the entrance of this restaurant was a sign, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Translate that for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It means Jews are not allowed to come in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was quite shocked me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you-- while you were working in the restaurant and while soldiers and civilians were coming backwards and forwards, did you have any idea of what was happening with the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the war? I had ideas through the old man. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The old-- which old man?

speaker: interviewee
text: The old man whom I gave the potato peels. He had a son in the military. And he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which military?

speaker: interviewee
text: German military. And he was a bit of a communist. And he listened to the English radio. And he always brought me results of what happened outside and so on. So I had the idea also towards the end, I could see it. The soldiers which came were already closing [INAUDIBLE]. And they were worn. And I knew the war couldn't look good for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they-- you can't imagine towards the end how it looked in the waiting rooms. They were lying on the floor. It was such a good feeling for me. [LAUGHS] You can't imagine it, what was going on with them. They were completely destroyed. That-- that I saw in them. And I thought, it can't be long. It can't be long. They must come here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with this old fellow whom I gave the peels, he said, you don't go inside. And I knew I wouldn't go to Germany. Can I stay here? Come to my place. When the time comes, when you have to shift, don't shift. Come into my place. So he--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that what I did. And-- and it was actually terrible. You know, everybody went the other direction, the German soldiers and the military. It was like the front then, the town when I went to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what-- which time are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been in January '45, beginning of January.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you worked as the cook now. You sent food to your brother. And you worked in this restaurant--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --up until around January of '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and by that time, the-- it was obvious what was happening with the war. The war was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. You-- you could see it. Actually, the war came to us. It was like the front line. And very shortly before, the owners of the restaurant said, Toni, Toni, take your things and come with us. We have a train into Germany for the whole people who worked at the station. We have a train into Germany. I said, yes, yes. But I've got to go and get my things. And that was the moment I disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as your-- as the Roters, as your bosses were fleeing back into Germany, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --decided to go the other way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was really as if I would have went to the front line, through the front line until I came to this fellow-- I forgot his name. I don't know the--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the old man who you gave the potato peelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I always potato peels. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he had told you, when the Germans go back to Germany, you come to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We always spoke about that, because he brought me the news. And so the worse the news was for the Germans, the happier I was. And the more we planned what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so there was this mass evacuation of people like the Roters who were Germans back into Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who fled-- fled back into Germany, right. They fled into more inside of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you had-- you were sitting in the middle of the fighting, of the war, the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. You know, when I looked out of the window, like you see here sometimes, when they make the signs with the airplanes [INAUDIBLE]. So I saw it and saw the bombardment. The first they make the line where to go, where to bombard, and then the things fall down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Oels actually bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It was the front line. I don't think so long I was in at the station, they didn't bombard the station. I always was worried about it, because there was a lot of bombarding going on. Mostly 12:00 when we wanted to serve the dinner, we had it on the plates. [INAUDIBLE] And we had to go in the cellar. And I don't know whether they were Russian or English planes, practically daily towards the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was this constant bombardment going on all around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And they always wondered I didn't want to go to the cellar. You know, I-- you get so cold blooded I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you weren't worried?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't worried, no. Because I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's stop there.
